content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

October 3, 2016

Mary Beth Barber
6052 Northcrest Cir
Carmichael CA 95608

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-16-201

Dear Ms. Barber:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest, Section 1090, or the doctrine of incompatible activities.

QUESTIONS

1. Would joining the board of a nonprofit that is a contractor to the state through a program that you had recently been coordinating create a conflict of interest under the Act?

2. If you became a board member, can the nonprofit reimburse you for travel and per diem costs associated with board duties?

CONCLUSIONS

1. Merely serving on a nonprofit board is not in itself a conflict of interest. However, you may have a conflict of interest in decisions that will financially affect the nonprofit under some circumstances as discussed below.

2. If the board on which you serve is for a nonprofit charitable organization, reimbursement for travel expenses and per diem payments would not be income. If the nonprofit is not exempt from taxation under Section 501(c)(3), but is a nonprofit under a different section, these payments would be considered potentially disqualifying income.

___________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-16-201
Page No. 2

FACTS

You have worked for the California Arts Council ("CAC"), a state agency, for the past 11
years. For the past three years you have been an Associate Government Program Analyst
specifically assigned as the Special Projects Coordinator. One of the key projects that you
coordinated is the Arts-in-Corrections pilot program ("AIC"), a joint effort between the CAC and
the Division of Rehabilitative Programs at the California Department of Corrections and
Rehabilitation. You were the lead for this pilot project and handled the contracting components of
the various nonprofits and small organizations that provided services in state prisons through the
AIC program since the spring of 2014.

You will be discontinuing your service for the CAC and moving to a new job at the
California State Library starting on October 1, 2016. Your first work day is Monday, October 3 at
the Library; last day at the CAC is Friday, September 30, 2016.

You have become an expert in the field of arts for correctional rehabilitation as a result of
this intensive experience at the CAC. Upon hearing of your impending departure from the CAC and
the AIC program, some of the nonprofit organizations that are current AIC contractors have asked
you if you would be interested in joining their boards. Being a board member for these nonprofits
would not include compensation but may include reimbursement for travel and per diem costs
associated with board duties. You stated that most board members contribute to the nonprofit to
support its cause.

ANALYSIS

As a public official,² you are subject to the Act's conflict of interest provisions. While
merely serving on a nonprofit board is not in itself a conflict of interest, however, you may have a
conflict of interest in decisions that will financially affect the nonprofit under some circumstances.
Under Section 87100, a public official may not make, participate in making, or use his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.

A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision within the meaning of
the Act if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one
or more of the public official's interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) Section 87103
identifies interests from which a conflict of interest may arise. Those pertinent to your question
include:³

•    An interest in a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment of
$2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)); or in which the official is a director, officer, partner,
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103(d)).

________________________________________

² The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply to "public officials." Section 82048 defines "public official" to
mean every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agency.

³ A governmental decision that will materially affect your personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities (or
those of your immediate family) is also considered potentially disqualifying. (Section 87103; Regulation 18702.5.)
According to the facts you provided, your decisionmaking in your current official capacity will not affect your personal
finances.

File No. A-16-201
Page No. 3

• An interest in a source of income to the official, including promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).)

Business Interests

A nonprofit organization is not considered a "business entity" for purposes of the Act. (Section 82005.) Thus, Section 87103(a) and (d) do not apply.

Source of Income

Since you will be an uncompensated Board member of a nonprofit organization, you will not have an interest in the organization as a source of income by virtue of salary. Moreover, the definition of "income" also excludes "reimbursement for travel expenses and per diem received from a bona fide nonprofit entity exempt from taxation under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code." (Section 82030(b)(2).) Thus, if the board on which you serve is a nonprofit charitable organization, reimbursement for travel expenses and per diem payments would not be income. If the nonprofit is not exempt from taxation under Section 501(c)(3) but a nonprofit under a different section, these payments would be considered income.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel
Legal Division
